title: Rush-woven table mat
subtitle: A square woven table mat from twisted bulrush in a chequerboard pattern
excerpt: A rush mat is woven from twisted bulrush or sea grass in a simple chequerboard pattern of over-two-under-two, producing a flat, durable mat suitable for pot stands and table use. The technique is simpler than willow basketry: no stakes, no border to work, just two sets of parallel twisted rush strands interlaced at right angles. A first rush-work project.
sourceNotes: Rush mat and chair-seat weaving techniques documented in Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) and British rural craft traditions; rush matting a common floor and table covering from medieval period onward.

content.text: Rush mat weaving is a flat-work technique that does not require the stake-and-weaver framework of willow basketry. Two sets of parallel twisted
rush
strands are interlaced at right angles: the
warp
is held taut and the
weft
is woven through in an over-two-under-two chequerboard. The key technique is the
twist
applied to each strand as it is worked; this compacts the flat rush leaf into a round cord that produces a firm, even surface.
What you need: materials

Dried bulrush or sea grass from a basketry supplier, dampened before use. For a 30 cm square mat you will need approximately 60 to 80 strands of 60 cm length, each twisted as it is worked. Bulrush is the traditional material; sea grass (soft rush) is more widely available and slightly softer in texture. Both produce a firm, durable mat.

Prepare the rush by dampening it fifteen minutes before use: fold the rush leaves, wrap them in a damp towel, and allow to absorb. Damped rush is supple and twists without cracking; dry rush snaps at bends.
What you need: tools

Secateurs for cutting rush to length. A bodkin for tucking ends and opening the weave to insert new strands. A flat work surface or a simple frame for holding the warp taut (a rectangular frame of four sticks tied at the corners, with the warp strands looped around opposite sides, works well).
The work
Step 1: Set up the warp

Cut 30 rush strands to 65 cm (5 cm longer than the finished mat in the warp direction). Fold each strand in half to find the centre. Lay the strands parallel on a flat surface, centres aligned, side by side, each about 1 cm apart. The warp is 30 strands wide and runs the full 30 cm mat width.

Alternatively, set the warp strands on a simple frame: loop the doubled strands over a horizontal bar at one end and hold the free ends taut with a weight or clamp at the other. The frame method makes even spacing easier.
Step 2: Weave the weft

Take one weft rush strand (65 cm length), fold it in half, and begin weaving from one edge of the warp. Twist the two halves of the weft strand together as you weave: hold the two ends in the right hand, twist them clockwise with the right thumb and forefinger, then pass the twisted pair over two warp strands and under the next two, continuing across. After each pass, give the weft strands another twist before the next over-under sequence.

At the other edge, loop the weft around the outside warp strand and begin the return pass. Offset the chequerboard by two strands: if the first pass went over strands 1-2 and under 3-4, the second pass goes under strands 1-2 and over 3-4. Pack each weft row tight against the previous one by pressing with the bodkin or fingers. Consistent packing tension is what produces an even surface.
Step 3: Join new weft strands

When a weft strand runs short, introduce a new strand alongside the old one and continue weaving with both for two or three passes before cutting the old strand short and tucking its end under a warp strand with the bodkin. On a small mat you will need two or three joins; plan them to occur in the middle of a pass rather than at the edges.
Step 4: Tuck the warp ends

When the mat is at the target length (30 cm), cut the warp ends to 3 to 4 cm beyond the last weft row. Fold each warp end back on itself and tuck it into the last two or three rows of weft weave using the bodkin to open a passage. Pull the tucked end snug. Work across the mat on both sides until all warp ends are secured.
Finishing

Leave flat to dry for two days. The rush will contract and firm as it dries, tightening the weave. No finish is needed; rush mats are used unfinished. For a pot stand, a circle of cork or felt can be glued to the underside to prevent sliding.
Variations
Use two contrasting colours of dyed sea grass for a two-tone chequerboard: weave alternate weft passes in each colour.
Scale up to a round mat by working a centre-out coiling technique with twisted rush instead of the flat-loom method.

attrs.intro: Problems with the weave and surface quality.

items.fix: Use the bodkin to push loose rows tighter after the weave is complete, working across the whole surface. For future mats, count the twist turns per pass and maintain the same count throughout.
items.cause: Inconsistent packing tension across the weft passes, or the twist was not maintained consistently.
items.symptom: The mat surface is uneven; some areas are loose and others tight

items.fix: This is a common beginner result. For future work, check the warp width every five or six weft rows and ease the warp strands back to the correct spacing with the bodkin.
items.cause: Weaving tension pulled the warp strands inward at the centre of the mat.
items.symptom: The mat is narrower in the middle than at the edges

items.fix: Dampen the cracked strand with water from a spray bottle and allow to absorb before continuing. Replace any strand that is badly cracked.
items.cause: The rush was not adequately dampened before weaving.

attrs.heading: Common problems
